titleEn: Retrofit or replace? Why the market for autonomous retrofit kits is picking up speed
titleZh: 改造还是换新？自动驾驶改装套件市场为何加速升温
summaryZh: 本文深入分析了农业自动驾驶改装套件市场的崛起趋势。由于劳动力短缺、设备成本上升及农场盈利压力，多家公司（如Sabanto、AgXeed、Nivavi）推出可将现有拖拉机升级为自动驾驶的改装套件，避免购买全新机器的巨额投入。行业专家指出，该市场正从利基转向商业主流，尤其在大田作物的播种、种植和喷洒环节前景广阔。同时，改装套件面临技术支持、农民技能门槛等挑战，但投资者已开始大规模押注这一方向。
translationZh:
多年来，自动驾驶拖拉机一直是农业领域的热门话题，但种植者的选择有限。如今，越来越多的公司提供自动驾驶改装套件——通过硬件和软件将现有拖拉机转变为自动驾驶车辆，而无需购买全新的自动驾驶机器。

多位行业人士表示，自动驾驶改装套件市场正从利基市场转向真正的商业类别。Sabanto公司创始人兼首席执行官Craig Rupp描述了一个转型中的市场：“农业自动化正从新兴技术类别转变为实用的生产力解决方案，背后驱动因素是劳动力短缺、设备成本上升以及农场盈利压力增大。”

他指出，特种作物是早期的试验场，在那里，重复性作业和劳动力限制使得投资回报率在广袤大田农业赶上之前就已显而易见。Rupp认为这种情况正在改变：“未来十年，我预计自动化将扩展到广袤大田农业，特别是在播种、种植和喷洒应用中，因为农民对技术越来越熟悉，且成功的商业案例不断积累。”

Rupp强调，最显著的变化将是从购买越来越大的设备转向部署更小、更高效的自动驾驶机器，这些机器可以长时间运行而不受操作人员可用性的限制。从这个角度看，自动化不再是一次性的技术采购，而更接近一种新的运营模式——Rupp认为这种模式能提高生产力、降低资本支出需求，并有助于解决长期劳动力短缺问题。

**更小的设备**

这一论点最近获得了资金支持。7月14日，Sabanto宣布完成由Leaps by Bayer领投的超额认购B轮融资，参与方包括Sustainable Forward Capital、InnoVenture Iowa、Fulcrum Global Capital、DCVC和Yara。这笔资金将用于扩大Sabanto的经销商网络、提高改装套件产量、继续软件开发，并加速在行栽作物市场的推广。

“我们相信未来的主力设备是更小的机器，”Rupp说，“我们的改装方法让农民能够将劳动力转移到更高价值的任务上，增加作业时长，最终专注于发展业务和提升利润。我们看到太多农场在经济压力下倒闭，而我们的解决方案为它们创造了公平的竞争环境。”

对于Leaps by Bayer而言，投资逻辑既关乎资本效率，也关乎底层技术。“今天的农民需要既能提高效率又无需大量资本支出的解决方案，”该公司农业风险投资副总裁Paimun Amini表示。本轮融资之际，Sabanto正将其平台拓展至新领域——公司最近增加了种植功能，并与Precision Planting和DICKEY-john监控系统集成，旨在兼容种植者已使用的精准农业工具。

**大型制造商留下的空白**

在欧洲，类似的动态正在上演。荷兰公司AgXeed的首席商务官Arjan Mulder认为，对自动驾驶解决方案的需求正在急剧上升，但工厂制造的自动驾驶机器仍然稀少。“在西欧，市场上几乎没有工厂预装的自动驾驶产品，”Mulder说，“但农民当然可以使用他们现有的拖拉机，只需通过改装系统就能实现自动驾驶作业。”

AgXeed开发了VCU（车辆控制单元），这是一种改装套件，可将配备TIM（拖拉机机具管理）的拖拉机升级为L2级自动驾驶车辆。Mulder表示，该套件特别吸引依赖外部劳动力的大型农场。“这些企业通常雇佣大量罗马尼亚工人，”他解释道，“他们只是希望确保田间车辆能完全按照农场主或农场经理的意图作业。”

Mulder指出了当前欧洲推动自动驾驶改装需求的两大力量：日益严重的劳动力短缺，以及机械除草应用的增加。“从广义上看，全球范围内的可用劳动力正成为一个问题。而且，不同任务的可用劳动力质量也在下降。”

AgXeed目前在德国有一台机器运行，平均每周工作160小时。“如果你想用驾驶员完成这个工作量，那会非常困难。”Mulder补充说，越来越多的农场报告他们无法留住员工。

AgXeed与Claas密切合作，后者是目前唯一提供工厂预装VCU解决方案的主要拖拉机制造商：Claas Axion 900和Xerion 12出厂即配备VCU，可在Claas终端上显示。Mulder预计其他制造商也会跟进。他设想未来的农场将运行多种车辆组合：用于运输等任务的常规拖拉机、自动驾驶拖拉机以及专用农业机器人。

**劳动力萎缩，现有设备庞大**

荷兰公司Nivavi的创始人Niels van Vilsteren认为，自动驾驶拖拉机现在对许多农民来说已触手可及，因为不再需要购买全新的机器。他指出这一机遇的规模：欧洲仅农业领域每年就销售约14万台拖拉机，而欧盟统计局的数据显示，二十年内欧洲农民数量将减少80%。这种组合——庞大的现有机器队伍和不断萎缩的劳动力——在他看来，创造了对节省劳动力技术的结构性需求。

Van Vilsteren还指出，主要拖拉机制造商在很大程度上留下了这个市场空白。“拖拉机制造商本身并没有推出这些解决方案。这就是为什么我们希望在现有的、已经优化的技术基础上增加自动驾驶功能。”Nivavi将其系统Bart首先在荷兰推向市场，因为荷兰对农业自动驾驶车辆的监管更为先进——尽管该公司的雄心不止于荷兰国界。

**支持与技能仍是障碍**

ThornTek的Jay Katupitiya预计，仅从成本角度考虑，改装套件将拥有持久市场。“我确信随着时间的推移，改装套件会有市场，原因很简单：改装现有机器要便宜得多。因此，可改装市场显然将瞄准现有机器所有者。”

但他警告说，仅靠可负担性并不能决定这一类别能取得多大成功。“这些改装后的机器可能是自动驾驶的，但要让它们投入运行并处理相关的配置、编程和维护工作，并不是农民的工作，”他说，“这类产品需要改装套件制造商提供大量支持，而市场的发展将取决于农民对这种安排的接受程度——他们可能不具备处理改装套件所需的技能、知识和经验。”

**不是一个市场，而是多个层次**

Tersus GNSS数字媒体总监Mark Chen认为，改装类别根本不应被视为单一市场，而应看作几个不同的层次，它们以不同的速度发展。第一层，也是目前最大的市场，是自动转向和精准导航改装——厘米级导航、田块边界和基本操作数据工作流。

第二层，即受监督的自动驾驶或针对特定任务的无人驾驶改装，Chen表示，在欧洲和北美已从概念走向早期商业化，涵盖车辆控制、任务规划、感知传感器、安全监控以及针对耕作、喷洒、果园和葡萄园作业、谷物车支持等任务的远程监督。

第三层，Chen认为，更多是关于车辆本身如何融入农场现有运营。“用户不仅会问车辆能否自动行驶，他们还会问系统能否融入他们现有的操作工作流，”他指出，ISOBUS兼容性、任务控制器、FMIS/FMS平台以及跨品牌数据交换正成为越来越关键的决定因素——尤其是在欧洲，农场和承包商通常运营混合车队和多个软件平台。

综合来看，呈现出的图景是：一个市场正同时被多个方向的力量拉动——一方面是劳动力短缺和设备成本上升，另一方面是庞大的现有机器存量，而投资者现在愿意大规模支持改装路线。
featuredReason: 本文全面分析了农业自动驾驶改装套件市场的驱动因素、技术进展和挑战，对智慧畜牧与精准农业从业者具有高度参考价值，尤其适合关注劳动力替代和成本优化的读者。
contentFull:
Retrofit or replace? Why the market for autonomous retrofit kits is picking up speed

 20-07 | Autonomous/semi-auto steering systems | Article

Sabanto believes the workhorse of the future is smaller equipment. Photo: Sabanto

For years, the autonomous tractor has been a topic of conversation in agriculture, but growers have had limited options. Now a growing field of companies is offering autonomous retrofit kits – with hardware and software that can convert an existing tractor into a self-driving one, without the cost of buying an entirely new autonomous machine.

According to several industry figures, the market for autonomous retrofit kits is shifting from a niche into a genuine commercial category. Craig Rupp, CEO and founder of Sabanto, describes a market in transition. “The market for agricultural autonomy is transitioning from an emerging technology category to a practical productivity solution driven by labour shortages, rising equipment costs, and increasing pressure on farm profitability”, he says.

He points to specialty crops as the early proving ground, where repetitive operations and labour constraints made the return on investment obvious well before broad-acre agriculture caught up. That’s changing, in Rupp’s view. “Over the next decade, I expect autonomy to expand into broad-acre agriculture, particularly in seeding, planting and spraying applications, as farmers become more comfortable with the technology and proven business cases accumulate.”

Rupp emphasises the most significant shift will be from purchasing ever-larger equipment to deploying smaller, more efficient autonomous machines that can operate for longer hours without being constrained by operator availability. Framed that way, autonomy stops being a one-off technology purchase and becomes something closer to a new operating model – one Rupp argues improves productivity, reduces capital expenditure requirements, and helps address chronic labour shortages.

A large existing fleet of machines and a shrinking workforce creates a structural need for labour-saving technology. Photo: SabantoSmaller equipment

That thesis recently found financial backing. On July 14, Sabanto announced an oversubscribed Series B funding round led by Leaps by Bayer, with participation from Sustainable Forward Capital, InnoVenture Iowa, Fulcrum Global Capital, DCVC and Yara. The funding is earmarked for expanding Sabanto’s dealer network, ramping up retrofit kit production, continuing software development, and accelerating adoption in the row crop market.

“We believe the workhorse of the future is smaller equipment”, says Rupp. “Our retrofit approach allows farmers to shift labour toward higher-value tasks, increase operating hours, and ultimately focus on growing their business and their bottom line. We’re seeing too many farms fold under economic pressure and our solution levels the playing field.”

For Leaps by Bayer, the case is as much about capital efficiency as about the underlying technology. “Farmers today need solutions that improve efficiency without requiring massive capital expenditures”, says Paimun Amini, the firm’s VP of Agriculture Venture Investments. The funding round lands as Sabanto extends its platform into new territory – the company recently added planting capability, and has integrated with Precision Planting and DICKEY-john monitoring systems, aiming for compatibility with the precision agriculture tools growers already use.

AgXeed currently has a machine running in Germany that averages 160 hours a week. “If you want to do that with drivers, that becomes difficult.” Photo: AgXeedA gap the big manufacturers have left open

In Europe, a similar dynamic is playing out. Arjan Mulder, Chief Commercial Officer at Dutch company AgXeed, believes the demand for autonomous solutions is rising sharply, but factory-built autonomous machines remain rare. ‘In Western Europe there is hardly anything available for the market that is factory-fitted’, says Mulder. “But farmers can of course use their existing tractors and simply get to work autonomously with a retrofit system.”

AgXeed developed the VCU (Vehicle Control Unit), a retrofit kit that converts tractors equipped with TIM (Tractor Implement Management) into level-2 autonomous vehicles. Mulder says the kit appeals particularly to larger operations that rely on external labour. “These businesses often employ a lot of Romanian workers”, he explains. “And they simply want to be certain that the vehicle in the field does exactly what the business owner or farm manager wants.”

Mulder identifies two forces driving demand for retrofit autonomy in Europe right now: a growing shortage of labour, and the increasing use of mechanical weed control. “We see, broadly speaking, that available labour is simply becoming a problem worldwide. Also for the quality of available labour for various tasks.”

AgXeed currently has a machine running in Germany that averages 160 hours a week. “If you want to do that with drivers, that becomes difficult.” Farmers, Mulder adds, increasingly report that they can no longer retain staff.

AgXeed works closely with Claas, currently the only major tractor manufacturer offering a factory-fitted VCU solution: the Claas Axion 900 and Xerion 12 come equipped with the VCU from the factory, visible in the Claas terminal. Mulder expects other manufacturers to follow. He envisions farms eventually running a mix of vehicles: conventional tractors for transport and similar tasks, tractors that drive autonomously, and dedicated agbots.

A shrinking labour force, a large existing fleet

Niels van Vilsteren, founder of Dutch company Nivavi, sees the autonomous tractor as now within reach for many farmers, since it no longer requires buying an entirely new machine. He points to the scale of the opportunity: roughly 140,000 tractors are sold annually in Europe for agriculture alone, while Eurostat figures suggest Europe will have 80 percent fewer farmers within twenty years. That combination – a large existing fleet of machines and a shrinking workforce – creates, in his view, a structural need for labour-saving technology.

Van Vilsteren also notes that the major tractor manufacturers have largely left this specific market open. “The tractor manufacturers themselves are not coming up with these solutions. That’s why we want to add autonomy to the existing, already optimised technology.” Nivavi is bringing its own system, Bart, to market first in the Netherlands, where regulation for autonomous agricultural vehicles is further along – though the company’s ambitions extend beyond Dutch borders.

Support and skills remain a hurdle

Jay Katupitiya of ThornTek expects retrofit units to find a lasting market simply on cost grounds. “I am certain that retrofit units will have a market as time goes on, for the simple reason that it is much cheaper to retrofit an existing machine”, he says. “Therefore, the retrofittable market will obviously be targeting the existing machine owners.”

But he cautions that affordability alone won’t determine how well the category succeeds. “These retrofitted machines may be autonomous, but to get them operational and attending to the configuration, and programming and maintenance work associated with them is not a farmer’s job”, he says. “Such products will need a lot of support from the manufacturers of the retrofittable units, and the development of the market remains to be seen by way of how acceptable this sort of arrangement is for farmers who may not have the required level of skills, knowledge and experience to deal with the retrofittable units.”

Supervised autonomy or driverless retrofit for specific tasks is already moving from concept to early commercialisation in Europe and North America. Photo: AgXeedNot one market, but several layers

Mark Chen, Director of Digital Media at Tersus GNSS, argues the retrofit category shouldn’t be viewed as a single market at all, but as several distinct layers developing at different speeds. The first, and currently the largest, is automatic steering and precision guidance retrofit – centimetre-level guidance, field boundaries and basic operation-data workflows.

The second layer, supervised autonomy or driverless retrofit for specific tasks, is already moving from concept to early commercialisation in Europe and North America, Chen says, covering vehicle control, task planning, perception sensors, safety monitoring, and remote supervision for tasks such as tillage, spraying, orchard and vineyard work, and grain cart support.

The third layer, Chen argues, is less about the vehicle itself and more about how it fits into a farm’s existing operations. “Users will not only ask whether the vehicle can drive automatically. They will also ask whether the system can fit into their existing operational workflow”, he says, pointing to ISOBUS compatibility, Task Controllers, FMIS/FMS platforms and cross-brand data exchange as increasingly decisive factors – particularly in Europe, where farms and contractors often run mixed fleets and multiple software platforms.

Taken together, the picture that emerges is of a market being pulled forward from several directions at once: labour shortages and rising equipment costs on one side, a large installed base of existing machinery on the other, and investors now willing to back the retrofit approach at scale.

 "*" indicates required fields

 

 René Groeneveld Correspondent for Australia
